content.full_text:
SACRAMENTO
1020 N STREET, SUITE 107
SACRAMENTO, CA 95814
(916) 445-4154

BRAD SHERMAN
901 WILSHIRE BLVD., SUITE 210
SANTA MONICA, CA 90401
(310) 451-5777
(818) 360-3186
FACSIMILE (310) 458-9088

CHAIRMAN,
STATE BOARD OF EQUALIZATION

MEMBER,
FRANCHISE TAX BOARD

SAN FRANCISCO
50 FREMONT STREET, SUITE 1409
SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94105
(415) 996-9880

June 4, 1992


Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Acting General Counsel
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

RE:  Request for Advice -- Mass Mailing Provisions of
     Political Reform Act

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

     Pursuant to 2 CCR section 18329, subdivision (b), the
undersigned, with authorization from and on behalf of Brad
Sherman, Member, State Board of Equalization, hereby requests
formal written advice regarding the mass mailing provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

     Do the two attached newspaper articles, both of which
discuss Mr. Sherman's opposition to the California "snack tax",
but which were independently published by separate publications
(specifically, The New York Times and the Paradise Post), qualify
as separate "items" which are not "substantially similar" for
purposes of the mass mailing ban set forth in Government Code
sections 82041.5 and 89001?

     If these articles are to be mailed, they will be attached
either to a "Compliments Of" card or a piece of stationery
(similar to the stationery on which this letter is written).
Either of these attachments will meet the requirements of the
"letterhead" exemption set forth in Regulation 18901, subd.
(b)(1).


_________________________

     ¹ Government Code sections 81000-91015.  All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.

I can see this is a very faint or nearly blank document image. The page appears to be almost entirely white with minimal visible text or content. There are a few small marks or specks visible in the upper right corner and one small mark in the middle area, but no clearly legible text is discernible enough to transcribe accurately.

[illegible]

Request for Advice to FPPC
Mass Mailing Provisions
June 4, 1992
Page 2

     Since the two newspaper articles are neither textually
identical (see Regulation 18901, subd. (c)(3)(A)(1)) nor
congratulatory of the mailing recipient (see id., subd.
(c)(3)(A)(2)), they are "substantially similar" only if they meet
the two-part test set forth in subdivision (c)(3)(A)(3) (see
Kersten Advice Letter, No. I-90-054, at 3).

     Regulation 18901, subd. (c)(3)(A)(3), provides that two
items are "substantially similar" if "[b]oth of the following
apply to the items mailed:"

     a.  Most of the bills, legislation, governmental
         action, activities, events, or issues of
         public concern mentioned in one item are
         mentioned in the other.

     b.  Most of the information contained in one item
         is contained in the other.

     Both articles address Mr. Sherman's opposition to the
California "snack tax". Both articles discuss: (a) the
distinctions drawn by the Board of Equalization between taxable
and nontaxable snack foods; and (2) Mr. Sherman's argument that
the tax is regressive. Thus, both articles probably meet element
(a) of the two-part test.

     However, both articles are textually distinct, and therefore
do not satisfy element (b) of the test. The New York Times
article is a lengthy, 25-paragraph feature story, whereas the
Paradise Post article is a shorter, 12-paragraph editorial.
Neither article can be said to convey "most" of the information
conveyed in the other.

     Indeed, a substantial amount of the information conveyed in
the New York Times article is not included in the Paradise Post
editorial. The New York Times article devotes 15 paragraphs to
discussing the food product distinctions drawn by the Board of
Equalization, a subject which receives only 7 paragraphs in the
Paradise Post.

     For example, only the New York Times article notes that
"full-size matzohs are exempt, but smaller matzo crackers are
not." The New York Times article also discusses the following
food products not mentioned in the Paradise Post editorial:
marshmallows, Ninja Turtle snack pies, chocolate-covered fruit,
candied apples, yogurt-covered nuts, and trail mix.

Request for Advice to FPPC
Mass Mailing Provisions
June 4, 1992
Page 3

     In contrast, only the Paradise Post editorial discussions
the treatment of pre-packaged pies and unpackaged pies. In its
final paragraph, the Paradise Post affirmatively
endorses the alternative revenue source favored by Mr. Sherman:
higher taxes on incomes in excess of $400,000. The New York
Times article devotes one sentence to a more general discussion
of this point, a sentence which appears in the 19th of 25
paragraphs.

     The textual differences in the articles "are more than minor
changes or alterations for the purpose of personalizing the
letters." Leidigh Advice Letter, No. A-89-227, at 2. Thus, as
the staff advised in the Connelly Advice Letter, No. I-89-244, at
2: "[A] letter with text that bears no resemblance to another
letter will not be considered 'substantially similar' merely
because the letters seem to address the same concept or idea."

     More fundamentally, both articles are independently written
and published in separate newspapers which are not under the
control of the elected officer who proposes to send the mailing.
This fact distinguishes the situation addressed in this letter
from virtually every other proposed mass mailing considered by
the Commission.

     Thus, the situation addressed in this letter is a far cry
from the situation addressed by the Commission staff in the
Kersten Advice Letter, No. I-90-054, where the staff advised that
even though three letters written by an elected officer "differ
in some respects", because "most of the information in the three
letters is the same", the letters were "substantially similar."
Id. at 4. In contrast, the attached articles were published in
separate publications not controlled by the elected officer.

     This fundamental distinction between publications controlled
by the elected officer and publications independent of the
officer is recognized by the Commission in Regulation 18901,
subdivision (c)(4)(D). This subdivision defines as "unsolicited"
(and hence, exempt from the mass mailing ban pursuant to subd.
(a)(4)) any "communication sent in response to an elected
officer's participation at a public forum or press conference, or
to his or her issuance of a press release."

     In subdivision (c)(4)(D), the Commission recognizes that
when an individual communicates with an elected officer regarding
a press conference or press release, that individual is doing so
outside of the "captive audience" situation to which Government
Code section 89001 is addressed. This is equally the case when a
publication not controlled by the elected officer chooses the
publish an article which features the elected officer.

Request for Advice to FPPC
Mass Mailing Provisions
June 4, 1992
Page 4

     Finally, I respectfully note that the requested treatment of
the attached articles as "not substantially similar" will allow
the Commission to draw a bright-line rule based on the factually
independent publication of the articles. Any other treatment
will inevitably be based solely on a comparison of the textual
content of the articles, and will inexorably force the Commission
to waste scarce resources evaluating the content of independently
published media articles. While such content evaluation may be
necessary with respect to letters authored entirely by elected
officers, it is unnecessary with respect to articles published
entirely by publications not controlled by an elected officer.

     Please do not hesitate to contact me at (310) 451-5777 if
you have any further questions.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Steven M. Kamp
                                        Legal Counsel to
                                        Brad Sherman,
                                        Chairman,
                                        State Board of
                                        Equalization
Encl. -- 2 articles
